source_title: #380 Four Hundred Pages of Warren Buffett and Charlie Munger In Their Own Words

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Senra
paragraphs.text: The book I want to talk to you about today is Buffett and Munger Unscripted, Three Decades of Investment and Business Insights from the Berkshire Hathaway Annual Shareholder Meetings. It was selected and arranged by Alex Morris. Since I think 1994, all of the Berkshire meetings have been broadcast, they've been recorded and broadcast so anybody can watch them. So he went through like 30 years, watched every single meeting, took copious amounts of notes and then what he created for you and I really, the way I think about this, it's a reference tool. So he separated it not by year, but by topic.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Senra
paragraphs.text: I want to start out with Charlie Munger talking about the importance of creating your own luck by following your curiosity and your intense interest. Charlie says, Warren says he was lucky to find Geico, but not every 21 year old was going to go down to Washington DC, start knocking on the doors of empty buildings to try to find something out that he was curious about. So we also made our own luck by being curious and seeking wisdom. Buffett adds to this. If you enjoy what you're doing, you are likely to get a better result than if you go to work with your teeth clenched every morning.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Senra
paragraphs.text: My favorite way that they ever said this is Munger said intense interest in any subject is indispensable if you're going to excel in it. Naval Ravikant really echoes this idea, especially in the age of infinite leverage. Naval said, if you're not a hundred percent into it, somebody else who is a hundred percent into it will outperform you and they won't just outperform you by a little bit. They'll outperform you by a lot because now we're operating in the domain of ideas. Compound interest really applies and leverage really applies.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Senra
paragraphs.text: There's another thing that's really important and Munger talks about having the benefit, both him and Buffett had the benefit of role models that they can emulate inside of their family. Munger says, I think we're helped because we came from families where there were some admirable people and we tended to identify other admirable people outside of the family. Inside the family, Buffett called his father his hero. Munger was very close with his father and his grandfather, Judge Munger. Judge Munger's sound judgment and financial strength really helped the entire Munger clan survive the great depression.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Senra
paragraphs.text: You pick an extreme example and ask, what the hell happened here? Warren says, I owe a great deal to Charlie in terms of learning a lot about businesses. I've also spent a lifetime looking at businesses, seeing why some work and why some don't. As Yogi Berra said, you can see a lot just by observing. That's pretty much what Charlie and I have been doing for a very long time.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Senra
paragraphs.text: Charlie Munger is telling us that you really should build yourself into a person that deserves what you get. Charlie says, I had a great grandfather. When he died, the preacher gave the talk and he said, none envied this man's success so fairly won and wisely used. That is a very simple idea. We want to have people think of us as having won fairly and used wisely.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Senra
paragraphs.text: If you're actually paying attention, you can spot opportunities in parallel industries. They were talking about, I cannot believe how we absolutely missed Google. Because they were using Google's ads at Geico and it was the most effective ads that they could possibly find. Munger says, I feel like a horse's ass for not identifying Google better and Warren adds. We had some insights because we were using Google ads at Geico and we were seeing the results produced. We were paying $10 a click for something that had a marginal cost to Google of exactly zero. And we saw the ads were working for us. We could see at Geico how well Google advertising worked. And we just sat there sucking our thumbs.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Senra
paragraphs.text: Warren Buffett says, the world is overwhelmingly short-term focused. Andy Taylor took over Enterprise Rent-A-Car and he grew it from $78 million in revenue to $24 billion in revenue. And he was asked, what allowed you to do that? And he replied with one word, focus. He continues, I'm not the smartest person in the room, but I have the ability to focus at a level that most people can't. And I can focus for very long periods of time. He worked on that business for 38 years.
